---
title: "Tokens vs. Humans: AI Sentiment and the Evolving Demand for Data Jobs in the U.S."

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

![](AIPhoto.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

## Project Scope

Does AI-related sentiment carry predictive information about U.S. data-job demand beyond what hard labor data already contains?

"Sentiment": the tone of how AI and jobs are discussed, scored from news coverage and Federal Reserve Beige Book reports.

"Data-job demand": monthly openings and employment for data-related occupations such as data scientists, analysts and statisticians.

The test: a forecast built only from labor history is compared against one that also includes lagged sentiment, and the winner is decided by out-of-sample error. I will look at monthly frequency from 2021 to the present which brackets the late-2022 arrival of generative AI into public conversation and provides prior sentiment for comparison. The aim is to measure whether the story about AI can be a predictor of the labor market, not to prove that AI causes the changes.


## Introduction & Motivation

The public conversation about AI replacing data workers is running well ahead of the evidence: headlines describe data jobs being automated away, while hard labor data shows only a recent and contested decline concentrated among early-career workers since late 2022 (Cite Literature). My project tests if that talk holds valuable information, asking whether AI-related sentiment can predict U.S. data-job demand beyond what the labor history already provides. 

The question matters because narratives have consequences. A story that runs ahead of reality can steer students out of a viable field and freeze entry-level hiring before the data actually justifies it. On the other hand, a story that lags means workers, educator and policymakers underreact to a genuine shift.

Answering the question allows for those various groups to act on a measured signal rather than just speculation. If AI signals reliably lead changes in data-job demand, they serve as an early warning for a workforce in transition. If they do not, that itself shows that the labor data already captures what the discourse claims to add.

---

## Official Research Questions

* **Primary Research Question:** *Does AI sentiment carry predictive information about data-job demand beyond what the labor data alone provides?*

* **Secondary Research Question:** *Did the public release of ChatGPT mark a break in U.S. data-job demand, AI-jobs sentiment, or in both?*

---

## Initial Literature Review

### Prior Work 1: Canaries in the Coal Mine? Six Facts about the Recent Employment Effects of Artificial Intelligence
* **Source/Authors:** Erik Brynjolfsson, Bharat Chandar and Ruyu Chen 
* **Core Finding:** Using high-frequency payroll data, the authors found that early-career workers aged 22 to 25 in the most AI-exposed occupations saw a 16% relative decline in employment (up 3% from the previous year), while older workers held steady. The authors found that the employment decline was concentrated in roles where Artificial Intelligence automates rather than augments their work. Around the time of the public release of ChatGPT was where these employment patterns appeared to start.
* **How it informs the project:** This work contextualizes and grounds the labor-outcome side of my question. It also serves the central tension of my research: that the displacement signal is real. I plan to test whether the surrounding AI signals lead or coincide with the observed employment decline

### Prior Work 2: How AI Forecasts AI Jobs: Benchmarking LLM Predictions of Labor Market Changes
* **Source/Authors:** Sheri Osborn, Rohit Valecha, H. Raghav Rao, Daniel A. Sass, and Anthony Rios, University of Texas at San Antonio (2025)
* **Core Finding:** The authors of this paper built a benchmark for forecasting sector-level job demand from text and structured data. They used the same Indeed Hiring Lab postings index that I analyzed under strict controls. In their findings, they discovered that a simple moving-average baseline was hard to beat. 
* **How it informs the project:** This paper is the closest existing work to my project goal and shaped my methodology directly. I plan to use time-ordered out-of-sample evaluation and to benchmark similarly to a naive baseline. What makes our projects different is that I plan on keeping my forecasting model classical and testing AI sentiment as an exogenous predictor rather than prompting an language model to forecast like they did.

---

## Data Source Orientation

| Data Asset Name | Source Agency | Format (.csv, API, SQL) | Row/Column Count (Est.) |
|-----------------|---------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|
| JOLTS Total Nonfarm Job Openings | BLS JOLTS via FRED | .csv (also API) | ~61 x 2 |
| JOLTS Professional & Business Services Openings | BLS JOLTS via FRED | .csv (also API) | ~61 x 2 |
| Indeed Sector Job-Postings Index (Data & Analytics + tech) | Indeed Hiring Lab | .csv  | ~9,300 x 8 |
| Indeed National AI-Mention Tracker (share of postings) | Indeed Hiring Lab | .csv  | ~2,700 x 6 |
